g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about blockchain technology, its core features that make it revolutionary, and its diverse real-world applications.
Blockchain represents a paradigm shift from centralized trust models to decentralized, transparent, and secure systems that can transform industries.
g.text: Traditional systems rely on central authorities (banks, governments) to verify
transactions. Blockchain eliminates the middleman using distributed trust.

g.data-narration: Blockchain has seven core features that work together: decentralization, immutability, transparency, security, consensus, distributed ledger, and faster settlement.
Decentralization means no central authority controls the network. Every node has a full copy of the ledger and can validate transactions independently.
Immutability means once data is written to blockchain, it's nearly impossible to alter. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block.
Transparency allows anyone to view transaction history on public blockchains. Security is achieved through public-key cryptography and cryptographic hash functions.
Consensus is how nodes agree on the ledger state. Three main mechanisms: Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, and Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance.
A distributed ledger is replicated across all network nodes. Every participant has an identical copy, ensuring no single point of failure.
Traditional bank transfers take days. Blockchain settles in minutes or hours, operating 24/7, dramatically improving efficiency in finance and supply chains.
Top applications: Cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin enable P2P digital cash. Smart contracts are self-executing agreements that automatically enforce terms.
Blockchain enables end-to-end traceability in supply chains - Walmart tracks food from farm to shelf. Healthcare uses it for secure patient records and drug tracking.
Digital identity gives users control over their credentials. Blockchain voting ensures tamper-proof transparency. NFTs verify ownership of unique digital assets.
Blockchains come in three tiers: Public (anyone can join, like Bitcoin), Consortium (controlled by group of organizations), and Private (single organization).
Benefits include enhanced trust through decentralization, increased security via cryptography, greater transparency, improved traceability, and reduced costs via smart contracts.
